fname: 2026 - You et al. - Amygdala stress-responsive ensembles mediate distinct susceptibility to negative stress-induced remote depression in adolescent and adult mice.pdf
abstract: Highlights • Adolescent stress enhances stress-responsive neuronal ensembles in the BLA • Adolescent stress delays PV neuron maturation and reduces their inhibitory control in the BLA • The delayed development of PV neurons is mediated by microglial phagocytic dysfunction • Modulating stress-responsive ensembles or PV neurons alters remote depression susceptibility Authors Yue You, Chang-Ting Lv, Peng-Tao Ni, ..., Nan Sun, Jun-Li Cao, Can Gao Correspondence caojl0310@aliyun.com (J.-L.C.), gaocan@xzhmu.edu.cn (C.G.) In brief You et al. show that adolescent stress leads to abnormal microglial phagocytic function in the BLA and delayed PV neuron maturation, resulting in impaired inhibitory control over stress-responsive neuronal ensembles and increased vulnerability to remote depression-like behaviors compared to adult stress. These findings suggest potential targets for treating adult depression. You et al., 2026, Cell Reports 45, 117281 April 28, 2026 © 2026 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Inc. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.celrep.2026.117281 ll Article Amygdala stress-responsive ensembles mediate distinct susceptibility to negative stress-induced remote depression in adolescent and adult mice Yue You,1,4 Chang-Ting Lv,1,4 Peng-Tao Ni,1,4 Shi-Jie Wang,1,4 Le Liu,1 Yu-Tong Song,1 Ze Hui,1 Yao Huang,1 Yao Liu,1 Kun Tong,1,2 Jing-Ru Hao,1 Nan Sun,1 Jun-Li Cao,1,2,* and Can Gao1,3,5,* 1Jiangsu Province Key Laboratory of Anesthesiology, Jiangsu Province Key Laboratory of Anesthesia and Brain Science, NMPA Key Laboratory for Research and Evaluation of Narcotic and Psychotropic Drugs, Xuzhou Medical University, Xuzhou, Jiangsu 221004, P.R. China 2Department of Anesthesia, The Affiliated Hospital of Xuzhou Medical University, Xuzhou, Jiangsu 221002, P.R. China 3School of Life Sciences, Xuzhou Medical University, Xuzhou, Jiangsu 221004, P.R. China 4These authors contributed equally 5Lead contact *Correspondence: caojl0310@aliyun.com (J.-L.C.), gaocan@xzhmu.edu.cn (C.G.) https://doi.org/10.1016/j.celrep.2026.117281 SUMMARY Adolescent stress increases susceptibility to adult depression, potentially by encoding negative memories in stress-responsive neuronal ensembles, though the mechanisms remain unclear. In this study, we use a social defeat stress model with Tet-off labeling to examine the effects of short-term stress during adolescence and adulthood on behavior and cellular changes. Adolescent stress generates denser, more excitable stress- responsive neuronal ensembles in the basolateral amygdala (BLA) compared to adult stress, suggesting persistent traces of early-life stress that heighten remote depression risk. Adolescent mice display underde­ veloped, hypoactive parvalbumin (PV) interneurons in the BLA, further suppressed by microglial phagocytic activity. Bidirectional modulation of BLA stress-responsive neuronal ensembles or PV neurons significantly alters depression-like behaviors. Overall, our findings indicate that BLA stress-responsive neuronal ensem­ bles mediate differences in depression susceptibility between adolescent and adult stressed mice, with mi­ croglial dysfunction impairing PV neuron inhibitory control. These insights offer potential targets for early intervention in adult depression.
tldr: 사춘기 스트레스가 편도체의 스트레스 반응 뉴런 앙상블을 증강시키고 PV 신경세포의 성숙을 지연시키며, 이 변화가 이후 원격 우울 감수성을 조절한다.

fname: 2026 - Zhao et al. - Dissociating executive prefrontal substrates following capsulotomy in obsessive-compulsive disorder.pdf
abstract: 7 Obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD) is characterized by widespread executive function 8 impairments linked to disrupted fronto-striatal circuits. Ablative therapies such as 9 capsulotomy show promise efficacy in treatment-refractory OCD, yet their effects on 10 executive neural substrates remain poorly understood. This study explores how OCD and 11 capsulotomy influence executive prefrontal function. 12 Twenty-three post-capsulotomy OCD patients, thirty OCD controls, and thirty-two health 13 controls (HC) were recruited in the study. Post-capsulotomy patients were recruited at least 14 6 months following surgery to allow for post-operative stabilization. Participants completed 15 three executive function tasks assessing distinct cognitive domains: the Extra-Dimensional 16 Intra-Dimensional (EDID) task testing set-shifting, the N-back task testing working memory 17 and flanker task measuring conflict processing. The EDID and N-back tasks were 18 administered during concurrent task-based fMRI, while the flanker task provided only 19 behavioral measures. 20 In the EDID task, OCD capsulotomy patients demonstrated greater post-error flexibility 21 towards ID versus ED shifting relative to both OCD controls and healthy controls, despite 22 exhibiting more ED errors. OCD controls showed less shifts after errors compared to HC, 23 which was not revealed in the capsulotomy group. OCD patients showed less neural 24 differentiation between ED versus ID in lateral and mesial prefrontal regions relative to HC. 25 Capsulotomy was associated with decreased pre-supplementary motor area activity to ED 26 shifts compared to HC, indicating impairments in neural reactivity to ED shifting. No 27 ACCEPTED MANUSCRIPT Downloaded from https://academic.oup.com/brain/advance-article/doi/10.1093/brain/awag132/8655190 by guest on 17 April 2026 2 evidence supported the influence of capsulotomy in the n-back or the flanker task. 1 Specifically, both OCD groups showed impaired working memory performance at high 2 memory load along with dysfunction in the whole frontoparietal network. In the flanker task, 3 impairment in dissociating congruent and incongruent conditions were found in both OCD 4 groups but not in the HC group. 5 These findings suggest capsulotomy specifically remediates error-monitoring and 6 behavioral flexibility through mesial prefrontal remodeling, while leaving working memory 7 and conflict processing deficits intact. This dissociation implies that cognitive flexibility may 8 represent a state-dependent process amenable to intervention, whereas working memory 9 and conflict processing impairments may reflect trait markers of OCD vulnerability.
